data.title: Palantir Obliterated Expectations And The Stock Sold Off: Mispricing At Its Finest
data.description:
Heading into the Q4 print, I was bullish on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), citing an irrational selloff driven by headlines and the company's high beta with the software industry.

I had a high conviction that the company would surprise the Street's models, which would lead to even more upward sales and earnings revisions for the next quarters.

Well, it seems that I was right with my thesis, as the company obliterated expectations and put out a Q1 guidance for the U.S. commercial segment that was 33% higher than the initial estimates.

That didn't seem to matter, as the irrational pessimism in software dragged the stock down after jumping as much as 11% on the day after earnings.

In this piece, I analyze the highlights in Q4 and some of the key growth drivers behind the blowout quarter and guidance. I advance in this introduction that one of the new offerings, called Chair Reaction, could accelerate the U.S. commercial segment this year, building on top of the AIP platform.

Overall, I remain bullish on Palantir, and I still own the stock, despite seeing some risks later this year if the likes of Anthropic or OpenAI go public. I discuss more below.

Q4 2025 - A Blowout Quarter

Let's start with the top line. Heading into the Q4 print, the Street projected $1.34B in consolidated revenue. That figure included $473.7M from U.S. commercial and $528.4M from U.S. government. As is often the case, Palantir obliterated those expectations. The company reported $1.41B in total revenue, including $507M in U.S. commercial and $570M in U.S. government.

From a guidance perspective, the Street was looking for $1.33B in Q1 and $6.28B for FY26 revenue. The company guided $1.534B in Q1 sales and $7.19B in FY26 revenue. Naturally, this prompted significant upward revisions in the Street revenue models in the days after the results.

The highlight was the guidance for U.S. commercial, which I think was the main driver for the upward revisions in the chart above. The company guided Q1 U.S. commercial revenue of over $3.144B, vs. the Street's $2.36B expectations. That's 33% above expectations!

On the bottom line, the Street was looking for 23 cents in adjusted EPS and $701.1M in adjusted operating income. Well, in line with the tradition, Palantir eviscerated these figures after reporting 25 cents in adjusted EPS and $798.5M in adjusted operating income.

What's Driving The Outperformance?

Without any doubt, the U.S. commercial segment.

This is clearly visible on the chart below, with a dramatic acceleration in growth in this segment since Q1 last year.

In Q4 specifically, the remaining deal value in this segment increased by 145% yoy and 21% sequentially. When RDV is growing faster than revenue, you know the company is in growth mode:

Looking at the company-wide TCV (total contract value), this metric increased by 138% yoy to $4.26B. Once again, above the 70% yoy revenue growth of the company.

From a product development perspective, the company introduced Chair Reaction. In my view, this is the key product to monitor this year. Why?

Just take a look at who this tool is built for:

Exactly, the picks & shovels of AI. Overall, I think Chain Reaction may be one reason the company surprised the Street so much with its Q1 guidance.

Looking at the U.S. government segment, I simply can't be disappointed with the 66% yoy growth reported in Q4 2025. However, what I like about the government segment is its outstanding performance outside of the U.S, with the U.K. being one of the strongest geographical regions:

Fourth quarter international government revenue grew 43% year-over-year and 9% sequentially to $160 million, bolstered primarily by our continued work in the U.K.

In comparison, the non-U.S. commercial segment grew by a microscopic 8% yoy in Q4. Which brings me to the next section.

Risks

The AI mania is mainly concentrated in the U.S. To be honest, I share CEO Alex Karp’s frustration from Q4 2024: Europe’s AI adoption still looks anemic, and it’s frankly disappointing to me.

Therefore, the growth story of the company is still limited to one region: the U.S. In Q4, the U.S. accounted for approximately 77% of total revenue.

So far, it seems that investors are not particularly worried about this concentration. However, in the long run, there is a risk that growth in the U.S. reaches a plateau and, without a tailwind from abroad, the growth story stagnates.

The Street is already projecting a dire slowdown in growth, with a deterioration from 74% in Q1 2026, down to 37% in Q4 2027.

Some growth investors may not love this setup. If Anthropic and OpenAI go public in the next 12–24 months, I wouldn’t be surprised to see some money rotate out of Palantir, chasing other pure-play AI stocks.

For reference, Anthropic projected sales to quadruple to $18B in 2026, with a $55B target in 2027 (3x). On the other hand, OpenAI projects to grow its topline by (2.3x) 130% yoy this year, targeting over $125B in 2029. In other words, the implied CAGR of OpenAI between 2025 and 2029 is about 77% per year.

Finally, there is the beta risk, given the selloff in software in the last few months. Just take a look at the chart below, representing the returns and breadth of the different industries in tech over the last 3 months.

For reference, Palantir falls within Software Infrastructure.

Valuation

Palantir is still an expensive stock, trading at 115x forward earnings. Now, that valuation is lower than what it was a few quarters ago, largely due to the selloff in the software sector and the expansion in the company's forward earnings (the E in the P/E formula).

Conclusion

Do I think valuation is why the stock sold off by over 35% since the highs in October?

Absolutely not.

I think the 35% selloff from the October highs was mainly driven by the irrational pessimism in software due to the "AI will replace software" fallacy. I already discussed my discontent with this narrative in my AppLovin coverage, so I won't get into details here. Overall, I don't think a company/startup/individual is going to successfully vibecode the AIP platform and steal Palantir's existing and potential new customers. It is my view that enterprise AI is a winner-take-all market, and the first few players to win are the ones that will take most of the market share. Regardless of the competition from OpenAI and Anthropic, Palantir is already seen as a clear winner in this space.

From a price target perspective, I think we could see new highs before the end of the year, likely in H2 rather than H1.

data.title: Brookfield Corporation And The Complexity Discount
data.description:
Whenever I talk about Brookfield Corporation (BN), I usually come across two types of investors. Type A investors find the company too complex to understand, put it into their 'too hard pile,' and never really invest in it. Whereas Type B investors look at its past performance or read articles that recommend buying Brookfield and choose to invest, hoping that everything goes well.

The reality is that only a limited number of investors truly understand Brookfield, its ecosystem, its structuring, and how it really functions. My goal with this article is to help you understand Brookfield and why I believe it to be a lucrative investment at today's price.

Investment Thesis

Brookfield is often misunderstood because of its multiple moving parts and 'complexity,' which creates a disconnect between its intrinsic value and how the market values it. This 'complexity' also creates an opportunity for investors who truly understand the business to buy below its intrinsic value.

I often see investors getting spooked by Brookfield's high P/E multiple, which at present is around 98x. However, for a business like Brookfield, the right multiple is not P/E but DE (Distributable Earnings), and I'll explain why later in this article. So, based on the DE, it is actually trading at a multiple of 18.9x, which makes it a lot cheaper in reality than the 'high price tag' that the P/E multiple suggests.

Not only that, but the company has also grown its Distributable Earnings per share before realizations from $1.26 in 2021 to $2.27 in 2025, which means that it has compounded annually at a 16% growth rate, and Brookfield's target is to continue compounding it at 15% for the foreseeable future.

Today, Brookfield Corporation's stock is trading at $48, so there is more than 31% upside relative to its intrinsic value of $63 based on its sum-of-the-parts valuation, and I see this gap narrowing partly due to the company's efforts to simplify its structure and educate more investors about Brookfield and broader coverage of the stock.

Business Overview

A good way to understand Brookfield Corporation is to think of it as a conglomerate that owns an asset management business (BAM), Wealth Solutions/Insurance business (BNT), and other operating businesses like Renewable Power and transition (BEP), Infrastructure (BIP), Private Equity (BBU), and Real Estate (privately owned). These businesses provide cash flows (distributable earnings) to the parent company, BN, which it then distributes to its shareholders through buybacks and dividends and reinvests the rest in areas where it sees the most opportunity.

Put another way, at the parent company/corporate level, the total Distributable Earnings are made up of Distributable Earnings from its businesses and its own investments, realizations of carried interest, and sales of its investments from its corporate balance sheet, minus costs and some other deductions.

For instance, in 2025, Brookfield's total Distributable Earnings (including realized carried interest and disposition gains) were $6 billion. The company spent around $1.6 billion on buying back its shares and dividends and then reinvested $259 million in the asset management business, $1.7 billion in the wealth solutions business, and about $2.8 billion in other operating businesses.

So, Brookfield Corporation's main role today is to allocate capital in the most efficient way to keep the compounding snowball rolling for a long, long time. And the numbers suggest that it is actually doing really well on this front. If we look at its total capital (net value) in 2021, it was $68 billion, but at the end of 2025, it was $180 billion—an eye-popping CAGR of 27.6%.

Revenues & Synergies

As highlighted above, Brookfield's businesses generate Funds from Operations and distribute their earnings to the parent corporation as well as other shareholders. However, the real appeal comes not from their independent operations but from the synergies that they create and the ecosystem that Brookfield has built, where growth in one business leads to growth in other businesses. For instance, if we think about AI infrastructure, Brookfield's infrastructure business builds data centers, whereas the Renewable Energy and Transition business provides the energy that is required to run them, perfectly complementing each other. Additionally, most of these businesses, like utilities, pipelines, and telecom towers, are in regulated industries, which creates a sort of barrier to entry and provides stable, inflation-indexed cash flows to Brookfield.

Similarly, the wealth solutions business brings in revenue and assets by selling annuities and other insurance products to retail and institutional customers and distributes almost all of its earnings to Brookfield Corporation. Not only that, but the wealth solutions business also invests in funds that are managed by Brookfield Asset Management, and this relationship is beneficial for both parties. While BAM gets to manage more fee-bearing assets, the wealth solution business gets high yield on its investments, widening its spread-related earnings. As an example, last year the wealth solutions business invested $13 billion in Brookfield's strategies with an 8.5% average yield, whereas the average cost of funds was 3.45%, which means more spread-based earnings for the business going forward. Last year, the net yield of the investment portfolio was 5.7%, leading to a gross spread of 2.25%.

Distributable Earnings (DE) Analysis

Brookfield recently released its Q4 2025 and Full-Year Results. For us, the most pragmatic thing to do is to focus on its distributable earnings, as this is a crucial metric to do a health checkup of Brookfield.

Now, there are reasons for valuing Brookfield based on its DE multiple instead of its P/E multiple. Firstly, Brookfield's operating businesses are asset-heavy, which creates huge non-cash expenses like depreciation and amortization. Secondly, let's say the value of an asset this year goes down; it will be reflected in this year's earnings due to mark-to-market rules, whereas in reality, the company might not even be planning to sell that asset for years. These changes can present accounting earnings in a way that might not accurately reflect the true economic reality.

Distributable earnings, on the other hand, represent the actual cash that Brookfield generated from operations as well as carried interest realizations and other investment sales. This metric avoids those accounting changes on paper and provides us with a clearer view of what's really happening with the business.

In 2025, even though Distributable Earnings before realizations went up by 11% YoY, and the full-year realized carried interest grew from $403 million in 2024 to $560 million, total Distributable Earnings actually went down by 4% YoY.

Now, this didn't happen because of weakness in the underlying businesses but because of the fact that in 2024, the company sold a 2% stake in BAM to fund the American Equity Life acquisition, and this sale added around $1 billion to the total distributable earnings, whereas in 2025, the company just sold $62 million worth of principal investments. That's why the total DE in 2024 was higher than in 2025, despite the underlying DE growth and more carried interest realization last year.

Also, at the end of 2025, Brookfield had $11.6 billion in unrealized carried interest, and the management in the recent earnings press release said that it plans to realize a significant portion of it over the next three years.

As an investor in BN, this would simply mean higher distributable earnings YoY if DE from its businesses continues to grow.

Valuation

Due to Brookfield's multiple businesses and moving parts, we'll use a sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation to get a sense of its intrinsic value.

According to Brookfield's own estimates, the net value per share is around $68, but I've made some adjustments for a more conservative estimate, and the adjusted value comes to around $63 per share. Now, if we compare this to its current stock price of ~$48, this represents a 24% discount to the intrinsic value.

Now, let's also calculate the intrinsic value using different growth rates. For this, I'll use per-share DE before realizations of $2.24 instead of total DE to avoid volatility caused by realizations and other investment sales. At $48, this multiple is around 21x, and we'll assume this as the multiple at the end of the 5-year calculation period.

As you can see, at a 15% growth rate for the next five years and a 15% discount rate, the estimated value is $58.24, which means the upside from the current price is around 21%.

So, even if we choose not to consider the SOTP valuation for a while, the stock is still trading below its intrinsic value based on our growth rate and discount rate assumptions.

Risks

First of all, commercial real estate (CRE), especially offices, which have been struggling with occupancy rates over the past few years, still faces significant headwinds ahead. In its 2026 Alternative Investment Outlook, even though JPMorgan expects the commercial real estate to recover, the recovery is expected to be based on asset quality instead of all assets at once, which means there might be continued pain ahead for Brookfield's value-add and opportunistic CRE (around 12% of the total portfolio) in the short to medium term. I think it's also worth mentioning that 82% of Brookfield's CRE portfolio is Super Core and Core Plus, with occupancy rates higher than 90%.

However, this doesn't take away from the fact that recovery is still going to take time, especially with most return-to-office mandates today creating hybrid workflows instead of the pre-pandemic five-day-a-week office requirements.

This also leads us to another concern with Brookfield, which is the ability to exit investments and sell them at attractive prices. What has happened over the past few years due to high interest rates is that firms like Brookfield, KKR, Apollo, and Blackstone struggled to monetize their assets due to a slowdown in M&A, IPOs, and deal activity. Now, the data does suggest that deal activity is picking up steam, but it is still below its peak in 2021.

While there are geopolitical and regulatory risks associated with Brookfield due to its businesses in regulated industries like energy, telecommunications, utilities, and tolls, it is also important to note that while Brookfield's operations are global, they are still local. For example, a telecom tower in India operates in India, and one in France operates in France. This, in a way, provides some insulation from things like tariffs.

Catalysts

The way Brookfield is positioned today, I see so many growth opportunities on several fronts for the business that support the bullish thesis for the stock.

Firstly, its asset management business would benefit greatly due to the increasing inflow of capital from individual investors instead of just institutions. Plus, the opening up of retirement accounts in the U.S. to alternatives would greatly benefit BAM and, as a result, the parent company.

Secondly, even though the market is concerned about AI spending, in reality, this spending is continuously going up, and this trend is expected to continue at least for the next five to ten years. Brookfield today owns three necessary ingredients for the AI Infrastructure buildout: Energy (renewable and nuclear), Infrastructure (100+ data centers), and Capital ($188b dry powder). More importantly, these are not areas that Brookfield has jumped into recently. The company has decades of experience in these domains, which differentiates it from new entrants. It's already benefiting from increased AI spend, as evident from its multi-billion-dollar, multi-year contracts with companies like Microsoft, Google, and Amazon.

Last year, Brookfield also launched a $100 billion AI infrastructure fund, in which NVIDIA (NVDA), the Kuwait Investment Authority, and other investors have already made commitments.

Even on the wealth solutions side, the business is growing rapidly as well, with demand for annuities expected to go up as more people age and retire. So, I'd say that there is a lot going on for Brookfield at this point, which will help it grow in the future.

The Bottom Line

To sum up, I'd say that the 'complexity discount' for Brookfield actually creates a great opportunity to buy a company that has significant growth levers ahead. Not only does it benefit from consistent revenue streams from regulated infrastructure and power businesses, but it also benefits from the growth of its high-margin, asset-light asset management business with significant 'fuel' provided by the float from its Wealth Solutions business.

All in all, despite the risks, the company is significantly undervalued today based on the SOTP valuation and the Growth/Discount model. I, therefore, assign a Strong Buy rating to Brookfield Corporation.

The Morningstar ratings for the overall, three-, five- and 10-year periods for Class A shares are 4 stars, 4 stars, 5 stars and 4 stars and for Institutional Class shares are 4 stars, 4 stars, 5 stars and 4 stars among 220, 220, 197 and 146 Technology funds respectively, and are based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure.

Fund strategy

  • Invests in technology or technology related companies, generally 50-75, across all market capitalizations, to diversify and mitigate portfolio risk. The top holdings are companies in which the team has the highest degree of confidence.
  • Selects investments based on rigorous bottom-up fundamental analysis. Our growth-at-a-reasonable-price (GARP) style aims to identify undervalued and misunderstood technology companies.
  • Analyst team averages over 20 years of investment experience and utilizes their industry knowledge to identify companies they believe have the most enduring intellectual property, the best management teams and the longest runway of high-profit revenue growth.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Seligman Technology and Information Fund (CCIZX) returned 5.09% for the fourth quarter.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P North American Technology Index, which returned 3.39% for the quarter.
  • Stock selection within semiconductors & semiconductor equipment; technology hardware, storage & peripherals; and electronic equipment, instruments & components contributed positively to performance relative to the benchmark. The fund's underweight allocation to software also contributed to relative performance, as did out-of-Index allocations to electrical equipment within the industrials sector and hotels, restaurants & leisure within the consumer discretionary sector.
  • For monthly performance, please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com.

Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% during the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The Index returned 17.37% in 2025, marking the third year in a row in which it gained 15% or more, as well as the sixth of the last seven years. The positive fourth-quarter return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year — better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a “bubble.” Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

  • Technology indices continued to move higher during the quarter as evidenced by the S&P North American Technology Sector Index which returned 3.39% during the fourth quarter. The quarter was not a totally smooth ride, and there were pockets of volatility that the technology market had not felt since headline tariff volatility in April. The primary driver of volatility during the quarter was declining sentiment in the "AI trade" and whether the significant AI capex spend had ramped up too fast for what the market was expecting. This led to a sharp sell-off in AI-indexed companies. However, the market ultimately settled, following a very strong earnings report from Micron that helped ease some concerns around the AI-capex race.
  • The fund maintained an underweight position in NVIDIA relative to the S&P North American Technology Sector Index. The chip manufacturing company still maintained a large allocation in the benchmark, at an average weight of over 8.4% during the quarter. The fund's underweight position contributed to performance relative to the benchmark. NVIDIA continued to deliver strong results, including an earnings beat during the quarter and an upbeat outlook. However, the market reacted cautiously following the earnings report, as investors digested margin compression and whether the future upward guidance had already been priced into the strong run the stock has had since 2023.
  • The portfolio's holding in semiconductor equipment manufacturer Lam Research contributed significantly to positive performance, as the stock performed exceptionally well during the quarter and the fund maintained a significant relative overweight. Lam continued to be an AI beneficiary from a company perspective, as AI workloads require more advanced chips, which drove demand for Lam's deposition and etch solutions. The market has shown potential for a memory "supercycle," which has led to significant investments in high-bandwidth memory and AI chips and is fueling demand for Lam's wafer fabrication equipment.
  • The fund continued to hold an out-of-benchmark position in Bloom Energy, which manufactures and markets solid-oxide fuel cells that produce electricity and can provide an alternative source of energy compared with traditional supply. Our thesis on Bloom has been that it has the technology to provide a solution to the electricity shortage that overhangs new AI-datacenter builds in the U.S. and around the world. Bloom's fuel cells plug into a natural gas line that can fit on a data center's campus without taking up much real estate, and the company has informed customers that it can ramp up power delivery quicker than other energy providers. Bloom's stock price did pull back at times during the quarter, especially as a group of higher-beta stocks faced heightened volatility behind the fear of an overspend on AI buildouts. We continue to hold high conviction in Bloom Energy, especially with the clear shortage in reliable power solutions to power AI data centers going forward. Jensen Huang, NVIDIA's CEO, has said multiple times that power is the largest constraint on AI; as a result, there has been a clear demand for power solutions that can be scaled up quickly and efficiently.
  • The fund maintained an overweight position in technology hardware, storage & peripherals company Western Digital. Early in 2025, the company announced a strategic spin-off separating its flash division as SanDisk, while Western Digital focused primarily on the hard disk drive (HDD) market, allowing the company to be valued as a standalone HDD leader. The continued surge in AI infrastructure demand massively boosted the demand for high-capacity HDDs, and Western Digital benefited from an increase in purchase orders from major hyperscalers extending into 2026 and even 2027.
  • The fund's allocations to semiconductor equipment companies Teradyne (TER) and Applied Materials contributed positively to performance during the quarter, following a strong overall period for semiconductor capital equipment companies. Teradyne's strong price appreciation was led by an increased demand for semiconductor testing equipment, fueled by booming demand for AI accelerators and memory. Its management team also reported strong results for the third quarter of 2025 and issued optimistic guidance for future business with significant revenue growth year over year. Applied Materials' management team emphasized the continued shift toward AI-related chip production, including foundry logic, dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) and high-bandwidth memory - all areas in which the company holds strong positioning. Applied Materials also expanded their gross margins during the quarter, citing some of the strongest margins it has seen in approximately 25 years.

Outlook

The market is navigating significant crosscurrents of positive and negative trends. Following two strong quarters for overall market returns, and technology specifically, volatility did increase slightly during the fourth quarter, but fundamentals remained strong overall. Positive market trends include:

  • AI infrastructure build-out remains strong, as hyperscalers and enterprises are committing to large-scale spends on graphics processing units (GPUs), high-speed networking and high-bandwidth memory.
  • Corporate technology refresh and digitization are intact, as large-cap companies continue to invest in cloud, security, data platforms and automation.
  • Storage and memory pricing and margins have improved, following a down cycle in 2022 and 2023. Hard-disk drives and NAND/DRAM markets tightened in 2025 on AI data demand, lifting prices and margins within the subsector.

The negatives include:

  • Semiconductor supply chains remain exposed to export controls and geopolitical tensions, given the current environment.
  • While AI demand is strong, graphics processing unit (GPU) and high bandwidth memory (HBM) costs are rising, tightening margins for cloud providers and downstream integrators.
  • Increased regulatory scrutiny on data privacy, AI ethics and antitrust could be a headwind for cybersecurity, as companies look for more solutions amid increasing AI adoption.

Our expectations for various sectors are as follows:

  • Favorable Fed policy with the potential for additional rate cuts could support capital spending and technology investment in growth segments like AI and semiconductors.
  • Semiconductor equipment demand remains steady and has continued to recover strongly, following increased volatility in April 2025 around global tariffs. Semiconductor capital equipment companies have been focused on reallocating production across geographic locations to adjust for potential impacts of tariffs if conversations restart.
  • Cybersecurity consistently remains a top priority for chief information officer (CIO) budgets, as non-technology-focused companies continue to increase the use of AI solutions in their day-to-day operations.

data.title: Guggenheim Municipal Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) (GIJIX) returned 2.14 percent in the fourth quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, by 0.58 percent.
  • Among large sector weights, school districts, housing, and general purpose bonds contributed positively to performance, while transportation detracted from performance.
  • Among the most-represented states in the portfolio, Tennessee and Oregon bonds contributed the most to total return, while those from New York and Texas detracted from returns for the period.

Strategy and Positioning

  • Municipal/Treasury yield ratios have compressed following the recent rally. The municipal curve has also flattened significantly from the summer months, outperforming the Treasury curve. We believe at current valuation levels, investors should be more selective on both credit selection and duration positioning.
  • We prefer high coupon structures with good call protection.
  • Higher education and healthcare sectors face pressure from federal funding reductions. Given the fragmented nature of municipal bonds and diverse security pledges, we anticipate these sectors will offer opportunities due to temporary mispricings.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

  • School districts, general purpose, and housing bonds contributed to performance.
  • Transportation bonds detracted from performance.
  • Tennessee and Oregon bonds contribute to performance.
  • New York and Texas bonds detracted from performance.

Economic and Market Review

  • Economic growth in 2025 was resilient despite policy uncertainty and a cooler labor market. Recent third quarter real gross domestic product (GDP) reading revealed positive underlying growth momentum driven by consumer spending and a continued boost in artificial intelligence (AI) investment.
  • Looking ahead, we expect the U.S. economy to move toward equilibrium in 2026 with growth stabilizing around 2 percent, supported by continued consumer spending bolstered by wealth effects from rising asset prices and AI investment. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) should offer fiscal stimulus in the first half of the year, as retroactive tax cuts expand household refunds and investment incentives broaden capex spending.
  • The labor market remains a downside risk to our outlook, and government distortions continue to make official labor market data unusually noisy. December data showed low hiring, but the unemployment rate surprised to the downside, falling to 4.38 percent, suggesting a stable market supply/demand balance. The labor market should further stabilize as growth remains solid in 2026 and is accompanied by solid productivity gains. However, shocks to the labor market remain a risk to the outlook.
  • Core inflation is likely to remain sticky in the near term as tariff passthrough effects continue to be felt, but should continue a downward trend in the second half of 2026. Fundamental drivers of inflation, including measures of rent inflation, continue to soften. Accompanied by a softer labor market and easing wage pressures, services inflation should also decrease. We expect year-over-year core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) to fall to around 2.5 percent by the fourth quarter.
  • Views on the path for monetary policy remain dispersed, reflecting different perspectives on neutral rates and the balance of risks to the Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) dual mandate. The Fed is unlikely to cut rates in the first half of 2026, though we anticipate two cuts in the second half of the year toward a neutral rate of 3–3.25 percent. Gradual easing should provide a stable backdrop for U.S. assets.
  • The Supreme Court’s ruling on the legality of International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) tariffs could weigh on markets. If deemed illegal, we expect a large portion of tariff revenue to be replaced by other sources, but refund payments would likely be delayed. The resulting uncertainty could impact growth and margins.

data.title: JRI: Back To A Discount After Announcing Rights Offering
data.description:
(Note: This article was previously published for members of Inside the Income Factory)

Closed-end funds (“CEFs”) can play an important role in a diversified income investment portfolio. There are several considerations to be aware of when evaluating a CEF for inclusion in your passive income portfolio. This discussion by BlackRock that was posted on Dividend.com explains several key considerations when evaluating a CEF.

  1. Understand the fund’s investment objective and portfolio composition
  2. Focus on total return when measuring a CEF’s performance history
  3. Assess the fund’s distribution rate and its distribution stability
  4. Analyze the fund’s premium/discount history
  5. Measure the fund’s liquidity and consider using limit orders

In addition to those considerations, I would also add several additional ones including:

  • Understand the size, reputation, and past performance record of the fund sponsor
  • Determine if leverage is being used appropriately to improve risk-adjusted returns
  • Pay attention to corporate actions that impact NAV, including share offerings

With those considerations in mind, I would like to take an updated look at a CEF that I last reviewed in June 2024. The Nuveen Real Asset Income and Growth Fund (JRI) delivered strong performance in the first half of 2024 and had just increased the distribution at the time, leading to my Buy rating. This was what I wrote in the summary of that article:

As a primarily income-oriented investor now that I am retired, JRI is very appealing to me, given the recent substantial dividend increase that brings its annual yield to nearly 14% at the current market price. Nevertheless, the fund still trades at a discount of more than -13% to NAV and the trend of the NAV is rising in 2024. Like other funds that invest in real assets that also significantly raised their distributions, the big bump up in the distribution is likely to have the effect of closing that discount, which will deliver additional capital gains to investors who buy now while the price is still relatively low.

Since that article was published, JRI has delivered a total return of more than 36%. Yet, because of the recent announcement of the terms of an RO (rights offering), the price suddenly dropped from a small premium at the start of 2026 to what is currently a discount to NAV of -7.27%, and the distribution yield is now 12.2%. I will discuss the RO more in the article below after I briefly review the fund in more detail.

CHICAGO, January 27, 2026 – Nuveen Real Asset Income and Growth Fund today announced that its Board of Trustees (the “Board”) has approved the terms of the issuance of transferable rights (“Rights”) to the holders of the Fund’s common shares (par value $.01 per share) (“Common Shares”) as of February 6, 2026 (the “Record Date”). Holders of Rights will be entitled to subscribe for additional Common Shares (the “Offer”) at a subscription price that is expected to represent a discount to the market price of the Common Shares.

While the NAV continues to roar higher in the first two months of 2026, the price has retreated to a much wider than average discount, giving me confidence in maintaining my Buy rating on JRI.

Nuveen CEFs

From a fund press release, here is a brief description of fund sponsor Nuveen:

Nuveen is a leading sponsor of closed-end funds with $53 billion in assets under management across 44 CEFs as of 30 Sep 2025. The funds offer exposure to a broad range of asset classes and are designed for income-focused investors seeking regular distributions. Nuveen has more than 35 years of experience managing CEFs.

Using CEFConnect (which is maintained by Nuveen, so I trust the data for its funds), we can clearly see the price action that took place once the shares started trading ex-rights, meaning that shares bought after the record date of February 6, 2026, are not eligible for the RO.

JRI Fund Description

From the fund website, JRI strives to offer enhanced income from investing in real assets:

The Fund seeks to deliver a high level of current income and long-term capital appreciation by investing in real asset-related companies across the world and the capital structure, including common stocks, preferred securities, and debt. Real asset-related companies include those engaged in owning, operating, or developing infrastructure projects, facilities, and services, as well as REITs.

The fund category is Hybrid Global Allocation, meaning that the fund holdings include both global equities (common and preferred stock) and fixed income (bonds, loans), with up to 40% of assets in debt securities. The fund’s inception date was April 2012, and it currently holds about $560M in total assets with leverage of 30.5%.

From the fund website, the hypothetical growth of $10,000 invested in the fund at inception shows the substantial difference in total return that can be achieved by reinvesting dividends. As of January 31, 2026, the initial $10k invested would result in $17,500 with no reinvestment and $26,259 with all distributions reinvested. This is an excellent example of the power of compounding, which is the foundational strategy behind my own Income Compounder portfolio.

Total returns by calendar year have been even more impressive in the past 3 years, with the potential for another strong year heading into 2026. The annual average returns of the past several years are shown on CEFConnect, and since 2023 the fund has delivered excellent total returns despite rising interest rates that hurt fund performance in 2022 and the Covid pandemic that impacted 2020 performance. The fact that Total Return on NAV so far in 2026 has been a robust 3.93%, while the market price Total Return has only been a negative 2.95%, shows how essentially “unappreciated” the past month’s underlying performance has been and why JRI now appears to be a bargain, with a much bigger discount than it was back in December.

Fund Characteristics

The asset allocation for JRI as of 12/31/25 shows about 25% in common stock of Real Estate companies, 23% in Infrastructure Common stock, and 20% in Infrastructure Preferred, with about 10% in each of Infrastructure Debt and Real Estate Debt and 8% in Real Estate Preferred. There was a total of 455 holdings in the fund at the end of 2025. The sector allocation is somewhat diversified, with top-weighted sectors in Electric Utilities and Pipelines, which are the ones most highly impacted by the surging global demand for power to support the AI data center buildout that everyone is focused on lately.

The top 10 portfolio positions as of 12/31/25 are shown on the fund website and include some familiar names such as Energy Transfer (ET) and Simon Property Group (SPG). Note that no single holding accounts for more than 1.5% of the total portfolio value.

Fund Distribution Rate and Stability

While I mentioned in the beginning of this article that Nuveen increased the monthly distribution for JRI shareholders in June 2024, it was a substantial increase of 34% to what is now $0.1335 per share monthly, where it has remained since.

According to CEFConnect, roughly 50% of the monthly distribution comes from ROC (return of capital); however, we can see that the ROC is not “destructive” to NAV because the trend has been an increasing NAV over the past year (after dropping to a low of $12.24 back in April 2025). Furthermore, many “real assets,” such as energy infrastructure companies, include ROC in their distributions, which get passed through the fund to shareholders. This structure can offer a tax advantage to those who hold JRI in a taxable account. According to the January 19a notice, 53% of the 2025 distributions consisted of ROC.

JRI Rights Offering

CEFs are “closed” in the sense that they cannot just sell new shares willy-nilly. They can offer new shares from time to time to raise capital, but they have to first file with the SEC announcing their intention and then notify shareholders. On January 27, 2026, the latest RO was announced for JRI. That announcement provided further explanation for the timing and purpose of this offering:

The Adviser believes this is an attractive time to raise additional assets for the Fund based on several factors, including the following potential benefits:

• Portfolio opportunities: attractive valuations in certain equity sectors provide the potential to improve total return performance for all common shareholders

• Tax-efficiency: potential to re-balance the portfolio to new opportunities without the need to sell existing portfolio positions, which may reduce taxable events for common shareholders

• Benefit for common shareholders: the Offer provides common shareholders with an opportunity to buy new Common Shares below market price

• Enhanced liquidity: the Offer creates the potential for increased trading volume and liquidity of Common Shares

• Lower expense ratio: the Offer is expected to spread fixed operating costs across a larger asset base

The point about tax efficiency is another good example of an Income Compounder or Income Factory type of strategy for funds held in taxable accounts. By taking advantage of the RO, investors in taxable accounts can use cash to acquire new shares at a discount to market price without the need to sell shares to capture capital gains.

Also, by raising new capital through an RO, JRI can make new investments without having to sell off some of the “winners” in its existing portfolio. That means those capital gains (which are included in its “total return”) remain “unrealized” for tax purposes, which is why they show up as “Return of Capital” and are untaxed as part of JRI’s dividend to shareholders. Sounds complicated? Here’s more info if you’re interested: Link here (https://seekingalpha.com/article/4493480-return-of-capital-for-dummies).

And even though the NAV is diluted for existing shareholders as a result of the new shares issued in the RO, by participating, common shareholders can lower their cost basis to offset that reduction in NAV. Furthermore, the overall expense ratio of the fund is lowered, as suggested above, by spreading those costs over a larger asset base.

The market price reaction to the announcement of the RO is typical, as the market price adjusts to a new, expected RO subscription price. In order to understand what the final subscription price will be, we need to examine the details of the RO.

Holders of Common Shares on the Record Date (“Record Date Shareholders”) will receive one Right for each outstanding Common Share owned on the Record Date. The Rights entitle the holders to purchase one new Common Share for every four Rights held (1-for-4).

The subscription price per Common Share (the “Subscription Price”) will be determined based upon a formula equal to 95% of the average of the last reported sales price of the Common Shares on the NYSE on the Expiration Date and each of the four (4) preceding trading days (the “Formula Price”). If, however, the Formula Price is less than 90% of the net asset value per Common Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the Expiration Date, then the Subscription Price will be 90% of the Fund’s net asset value per Common Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the Expiration Date. The Rights offering will expire at 5:00 p.m., Eastern time, on March 9, 2026 (the “Expiration Date”).

To summarize, if you held 400 shares of JRI as of the record date of February 6, 2026, you would have received 400 rights in your brokerage account with a ticker symbol of JRIRT (at least that is what it shows in my Fidelity account). If you decide to subscribe to the RO, you would be eligible to buy 100 shares (1 share for 4 rights) based on the subscription price on March 9, which will be the higher of 95% of the market price or 90% of the NAV.

Using today’s JRI market price of $13.16, 95% = $12.50. Today’s NAV of $14.16 at 90% would = $12.74. Therefore, the higher of the two would be used as the final subscription price, so it would be $12.74 as of today, February 12, 2026. However, we also need to factor in two distribution payments that existing shareholders will receive but the newly issued RO shares will not be eligible for. This detail is further explained in the RO press release:

The Fund has declared or expects to declare monthly distributions payable on March 2, 2026 and April 1, 2026, with record dates of February 13, 2026 and March 2, 2026, respectively. Any Common Shares issued after such record dates as a result of the Rights offering will not be record date Common Shares for the Fund’s monthly distributions to be paid on March 2, 2026 and April 1, 2026 and will not be entitled to receive such distributions. Shares issued pursuant to the Offer will be entitled to receive the monthly distribution expected to be payable on May 1, 2026.

Thus, if we add back the two distributions of $0.1335 or $0.267 to the NAV, we get $12.98 for the subscription price (90% of $14.43) based on current values, which will change in the next three weeks.

The point of all this math is that it is mostly a guessing game as to whether it is worth buying shares now on the open market at the current discount or waiting for a better price after the RO expires, which is what often happens as investors digest the effects of dilution. It is also a way of documenting the specifics of the RO for my own future reference as a JRI shareholder.

And if all the math in predicting an RO subscription price makes your head spin, another option is simply selling the rights and just letting the market determine what they’re worth. Existing owners (or new ones) can then buy additional shares in the market. You still have the choice of deciding whether to buy now or let the price drop further (maybe?) between now and the exercise date.

Summary: Buy or Add JRI at Discounted Price

'It's tough to make predictions, especially about the future.' -- Yogi Berra

It is difficult to predict the future price action of any CEF, and when a Rights Offering enters the mix, the math gets even more complicated. My review of JRI suggests that the fund is a Buy at the current discounted market price, currently -7%, but could be an even better buy for existing shareholders who decide to participate in the RO, depending on what happens to the NAV and market price in the next three weeks. The current trend shows a rising NAV, and if that continues, it might be wiser to buy shares now before the discount closes further (as the market price “catches up” to NAV).

Either way, if you are an existing JRI shareholder or just watching from the sidelines, I believe that now is a good time to buy or add shares of this high-yielding income fund that has a rising NAV and a wider-than-average discount.

data.title: Sandisk's Rally Tests A New Playbook
data.description:
I've been watching this cycle for Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) long enough to know how it ends. The demand increases. The prices rise. The cash flows soar. The management teams become confident again. And then, quietly, the capital discipline evaporates. What made this most recent quarter interesting wasn’t that it followed that pattern. It was that this management team was trying to break it.

What this most recent earnings announcement said wasn’t that this was a company that was benefiting from a cyclical upswing. What it said was that this was a company that was trying to prove it would survive one. The difference between those two things shouldn’t be lost on you.

NAND demand growth has been compounding at double-digit rates for a number of years, first with smartphones, then cloud infrastructure, and now AI. Structural issues with the market have rarely been the problem.

The problem has been the behavior of the supply side. When prices go up, the response of the supply side is to add capacity aggressively, lift the bit growth targets, and accelerate capex only to see the additional supply materialize as the market normalizes. Sandisk was a participant in this cycle before. The difference this time is the company's reluctance to lift the bit growth targets despite the mid-60% gross margin guide.

That’s the context in which I think about Sandisk. The recent re-rating of this stock wasn’t a vote on AI storage. It was a vote on whether or not this management team can overcome one flaw that has repeatedly destroyed value within this industry.

NAND’s Real Problem: Too Much Growth, Too Fast

NAND’s problem has never been one of technological obsolescence. NAND’s problem has been one of capital discipline. When NAND pricing gets favorable, NAND companies have time and again responded by increasing supply too quickly, trying to gain market share, and locking in costs that assume that environment will continue. When demand returns to normal, those companies are left with terrible margins, terrible balance sheets, and terrible results.

This isn’t a theoretical discussion. This has happened time and again. This has happened across multiple decades. This has happened across multiple management teams. In this environment, Sandisk’s Q2 results were unusual not for the size of the numbers but for how management talked about them. There was no victory lap, no need to accelerate supply, and no effort to declare the business de-cyclical. There was instead language that was conservative, even humble.

The Quarter Was Strong but the Capital Choices Were Stronger

From a financial perspective, the quarter was very strong. Revenue grew approximately 31% sequentially to $3 billion. Non-GAAP gross margins reset to 51%, and operating margins expanded considerably. Non-GAAP EPS was $6.20, considerably better than expected. Revenue for the following quarter is expected to be even stronger, growing to a range of $4.4 billion to $4.8 billion, with gross margins expected to be in the mid-60s. On paper, that is peak cycle performance.

However, what management chose to do with the balance sheet was even more noteworthy. Sandisk generated approximately $843 million in free cash flow during the quarter, using that cash to further de-risk the balance sheet. Debt was reduced considerably, and the balance sheet ended the quarter at a net cash position.

What management did not discuss was perhaps even more important, however. There was no discussion of accelerating capital expenditures, no suggestion that the strength of end demand justified revisiting long-term assumptions for bit growth. In this industry, that is what discipline during strong periods of end demand looks like.

Supply Discipline, Not Demand Forecasting, is the Experiment

There has been considerable debate about AI-related storage demand, much of it driven by discussions of AI-related storage demand. This is real; storage content in the data center is growing, inference is storage-intensive, and enterprise SSDs are accelerating.

While AI-related storage is real, it is not the experiment that is most noteworthy at Sandisk. The experiment is supply discipline, not forecasting end demand. The balance sheet is de-risked, free cash flow is strong, and management is not discussing revisiting long-term assumptions for bit growth, even in the face of strong end demand. However, demand has never been NAND’s limiting factor. On the contrary, supply discipline has.

However, the real experiment is whether they can align their capital deployment with long-term economics rather than short-term pricing. They were very clear that they are not willing to increase capacity unless they have long-term visibility into returns. That is underscored by their need to develop multi-year planning models with their customers, rather than just doing quarterly transactions.

But what is particularly interesting is that this is not a growth initiative. It is a risk management initiative. That is a very subtle, but very significant, shift in their mindset. If they are successful, the margin compression through the cycle may not be as violent as it has been in past cycles. If they are unsuccessful, they will just revert to the mean.

Looking forward, the big unknown is whether the bit supply growth in the mid- to high teens holds in the face of much faster exabyte demand growth driven by the build-out of the data center market. If the bit supply growth holds, then the price stability might extend further out in time, perhaps even through 2026.

If the industry responds to the high profitability environment by growing its bit supply even faster, then the traditional oversupply pattern might re-establish in late 2026 or 2027. The sustainability of this cycle will likely have less to do with the enthusiasm for AI demand growth and much more to do with the capital discipline in the industry.

Valuation is Compressing for a Reason

Yes, SNDK has had an incredible run lately, up more than 10x in the past six months, but the valuation still makes sense. SNDK’s consensus EPS forecast for FY26 is around $39 which equates to a mid-teens forward earnings multiple and drops to 7.7x using the EPS forecast for FY27 of $80, justifying the bullish rating.

Those earnings presume the margin structure will persist. Earnings that are guided to the mid-60s gross margins presume tight supply, mix, and price discipline. Assuming that is the case, Sandisk is a bargain. Assuming it is not, the current valuation is predicated on peak profitability.

The forward EV/EBITDA at the low teens is reasonable only if the capital model has indeed shifted. Historically, NAND stocks are cheapest when the timing of earnings is most fleeting. The valuation argument, therefore, is not one of multiples; it is one of behavior. As Sandisk demonstrates restraint in a normalizing environment, the floor to the valuation increases. As Sandisk fails to do so, the current valuation compression is an illusion.

Competitive Pressure is the Underappreciated Risk

The risks most often cited as risks to Sandisk are the ability to scale long-term agreements. I believe the risk is different. The hyperscalers have negotiating power, and they have it now. They have it now because as storage becomes more critical, it also becomes more visible as a cost component. They will negotiate price as supply normalizes, regardless of how critical NAND is to their architectures.

However, the Korean players are not observers and will react to price stability with competitive actions as supply normalizes. Yield, technology advancements, and capacity will help supply normalize faster than investors think. Sandisk needs to demonstrate restraint, and it needs to demonstrate it now. However, it will not be enough to change the behavior of the entire NAND industry.

Finally, there is technology risk, and it is the risk that is most often overlooked as investors discuss the financials and the long-term agreements. The technology risk is that the transition to newer nodes and the adoption of QLC are not as easy as the models presume.

However, if the rate at which enterprise SSDs are growing or the rate at which QLC flash is being adopted does not live up to expectations, the margin profile that the current forecast implies will be difficult to defend if normalization in pricing happens concurrently. This is not a bearish argument. It is an argument that reminds us that operating discipline is required to support current earnings levels.

Bottom Line

What keeps my attention is not the AI story or the company’s earnings. It is the change in behavior that management is attempting to formalize. Memory stocks rarely go wrong due to a lack of end demand. Memory stocks go wrong due to a lack of discipline. Sandisk is announcing a recognition of this history and an effort to act accordingly. The test will be whether they can maintain this discipline over the next 12 to 24 months as prices remain strong.

data.title: Rivian Q4: The Much-Awaited Inflection Is Finally Here
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Rivian (RIVN), I initiated a cautious “buy” rating on the stock. In the post, I wrote that while new investors could initiate a small position in the company with a potential upside of 50% to Wall Street’s highest price target of $25 per share, they should be prepared to weather a potential 20% downside risk in the short term.

Since the time of my writing, the stock indeed dropped close to 20% at the start of the month before it found support and bounced 26% higher on its Q4 FY25 earnings print.

For FY26, Rivian management guided for their full-year deliveries to grow 52% YoY, after declining two consecutive years, as they are set to launch R2 in Q2, with production to ramp in H2, marking an inflection point in the company’s growth trajectory.

While the launch of R2 will likely impede gross margins in Q2 and Q3, especially after the company posted its first full year of gross profit in FY25, markets are happy to look past it, especially as the company has sufficient liquidity runway to bring R2 to market after taking both capex and EBITDA losses into account.

In all of this, the demand conversion of R2 is still unknown, while supply chain bottlenecks could impede production ramp-up in H2. However, in the short term, the stock has closed above its 50D moving average, overcoming a critical upward resistance level and marking the return of investor optimism with much better than expected forward growth projections for vehicle deliveries in FY26.

I will continue to reiterate my “buy” rating at these levels but will point out the risk of elevated volatility that investors need to be prepared for, as the company now needs to prove its growth story by actually delivering at or above its targets in FY26.

Better Than Expected Revenue, With a Drive Towards Profitability

Rivian reported its Q4 FY25 earnings last week, where it beat estimates for both revenue and earnings per share by 1.83% and 21.04%, respectively.

On the revenue front, Q4 revenues declined 26% YoY, while for the full year FY25 revenue grew just 8% YoY, down from 12.09% in the previous year.

However, the slowdown in revenue growth was already expected, as management had made a strategic choice to pull back production of their high-end R1 platform so that they can retool their assembly lines for the more affordable R2 electric SUV in 2026, as I discussed in my previous post.

In terms of revenue composition in Q4, their Automotive revenues slowed down at a faster rate of 45% YoY to $839M, driven by a $270M decrease in regulatory credit sales, lower vehicle deliveries with the expiration of tax credits, and a lower average sales price.

On the other hand, their Software and Services segment posted a positive revenue growth of 109% YoY to $447M. On a YoY basis, the revenue contribution from their Software and Services segment has expanded from just over 12% in FY24 to close to 35% in FY25, driven primarily by their increased vehicle electrical architecture and software development services from the joint venture with Volkswagen Group.

The expansion in revenue contribution from the Software & Services segment is one of the most important reasons why Rivian achieved its first full year of positive gross profit in FY25, totaling $144M, up from a loss of $(1.2)B last year. This is because Rivian’s Software and Services segment carries a significantly higher margin compared to its Automotive segment, which continues to carry negative gross margins. Having said that, management is doubling down on operational efficiency for its Automotive segment, where it was able to deliver a $7200 improvement in cost of goods sold per vehicle during the quarter, driven by reduction in material costs, operational efficiencies, and product mix.

Moving on to operating profitability, the company continued to operate at a loss on both a GAAP and non-GAAP basis. On a YoY basis, their Adjusted EBITDA margins further contracted both for Q4 and for the full year, but this was primarily a result of the decline in revenue growth.

Forward Guidance Points to a Major Inflection

But it was not so much about the better-than-expected results in Q4 that drove the stock 26% higher after earnings.

Instead, it was their forward guidance, where they projected to deliver between 62,000 and 67,000 vehicles across R1, R2, and commercial vans in FY26. Not only did this number come higher than analyst expectations, but this marks a 52% YoY jump from FY25 delivery numbers, especially after two consecutive years of decline.

In the company’s Shareholder Letter, management also shared the timeline of their R2 launch, where they expect first deliveries to begin in Q2, with a growing ramp in the second half of the year.

As I explained in my previous post, their R2 electric SUV is priced starting at $45,000 and is targeted to capture the mass market, competing with Tesla’s (TSLA) Model Y and Ford’s (F) Mustang Mach-E. This is how the management positioned R2 during the earnings call below.

“R2 is an extension of the experience we delivered in R1 with design elements and performance to inspire adventure, but in a smaller form factor and importantly, at an attractive lower price point." The R2 Launch Edition will feature "a dual motor all-wheel drive setup that provides more than 650 horsepower and over 300 miles of range."

Note that Rivian’s R2 will include its third-generation autonomy platform, which contains two RAP1 (Rivian Autonomy Processor) chips capable of processing 5B pixels per second, along with their Universal Hands Free capabilities and Rivian Assistant, which will be launched in early 2026.

In early February, Rivian held a small event in Irvine where they invited a small group of media and influencers to take a first look at R2. During the event, the attendees drove a Dual-Motor R2, which is their Launch Edition in Q2, and shared positive feedback on the vehicle, its features, and its performance.

With deliveries projected to climb 52% YoY, we can see that revenue is also expected to inflect higher in FY26 to 27%, a growth rate that will not slow down at least until FY28, as can be seen below.

Growth First, Margins Later

Yet, in all of this, it is important to note that Rivian is unprofitable and is expected to remain so at least until FY30. Not to mention that its R2 launch will likely cause a temporary return to negative gross margins in H2 FY26. Per management’s guidance, the company is expected to post an Adjusted EBITDA of $(2.10)B to $(1.8)B, which would represent a margin of roughly (30)%.

Plus, in FY26, Rivian is expected to continue expanding its capex by 17% YoY to $1.95B-$2.05B. As of the latest quarter, Rivian has approximately $6.1B in cash and short-term investments. Plus, it is also scheduled to receive $2B from its Volkswagen joint venture through 2026 with certain contingencies in place.

With the above forecasted capex investments and EBITDA loss, analysts are projecting a total cash burn of roughly $3.5-5B for FY26. Therefore, given its cash position at the moment, the company has a meaningful runway to get R2 to market while ramping up production at the same time.

Assessing Rivian’s Investment Thesis

In my previous post, I had pointed to Rivian’s forward price-to-sales ratio and claimed that it was significantly “cheaper” compared to Tesla but argued that it is probably warranted given that Tesla is almost 20x the size of Rivian with far better unit economics.

At the same time, I also looked at Wall Street estimates, where the stock was trading at just 1.74% away from its average price target at the time, while the highest price target represented a potential upside of 50%.

As a result, I had rated the stock a cautious “buy” at that time. This is how I had framed my investment thesis from a technical standpoint below.

“The way I see it, while the stock could climb all the way up to Wall Street’s highest price target of $25 per share based on the upper end of the technical chart, it may come down in the short term towards the $13.50 level given its downward momentum and the convergence with its support levels. This means that while there could be a potential upside of 50% from current levels, investors also need to be prepared to handle a 20% or so volatility in the short term.”

Since the time of my writing, the stock indeed went down all the way to $13.73 earlier this month before finding support and bouncing higher after earnings.

In the meantime, the stock’s forward price-to-sales ratio has declined 15% and currently sits at $3.20, while Wall Street estimates for price targets haven’t fundamentally changed.

In all of this, Rivian has guided for a 52% YoY growth in vehicle deliveries, which is substantial in my opinion. Plus, both Wedbush Securities and Needham analysts are optimistic about the outlook, where they see Rivian optimizing their R1 production while ramping up R2 and simultaneously improving their unit economics through a combination of operational efficiency measures and Software and Services segment contributing to a larger share of Total revenue.

The way I see it, Rivian’s Q4 marked an inflection point not only from a fundamental perspective but also from a technical analysis standpoint, with the stock closing above its 50D moving average, which was one of the critical levels of upward resistance.

I continue to believe that the stock will head higher towards the higher end of Wall Street estimates at $25 per share, representing an upside of 40% from current levels. While the path towards the price target is difficult to predict in the short term, I believe the stock has cleared its first hurdle with a much better than expected forward growth projection that has instilled investor confidence back in the stock.

Risks and Conclusions

I will continue to rate the stock a “buy” given its improved fundamental and technical outlook at the moment.

Having said that, it is important to keep in mind that the price action towards the $25 will likely be quite volatile, which investors need to be prepared for. Aside from the company operating at a loss, there could be several other factors that could weigh on the fundamental outlook in the short term.

During the earnings call, the management already cautioned that the launch of R2 will impede gross margins in the short term (Q2 and Q3) before it turns into a benefit from production ramp in Q4. Should there be a delay in the production ramp from various supply chain constraints and capacity issues, this could translate to a longer period of margin pressures, which is currently not accounted for.

Plus, there is the demand side of the equation, where the R2 backlog needs to be successfully converted into revenue growth. If demand fails to meet expectations, this could quickly turn into an excess inventory issue, leading to further top and bottom line pressures.

These are all factors that both existing and new investors should take into account to determine potential allocation sizing and average cost basis for the stock as an investment candidate.

data.title: Diamond Hill International Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly Snapshot

Performance

The Strategy returned 3.87% (net of fees) and the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index returned 5.05%.

Key Drivers

Stock selection in health care and financials detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in information technology and industrials contributed, along with our overweight to communication services.

Summary

Periods of volatility were masked by strong returns for international equities in Q4 and throughout 2025. We were unable to keep pace with the benchmark, but took advantage of the volatility as disciplined investors who look for situations where prices diverge from intrinsic value.

Market and portfolio review

International equities advanced in Q4, with the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index rising 5.05%. Performance varied across regions – Europe led with a 6.24% gain, supported by strength in Switzerland and the UK, while Asia and Pacific markets lagged the benchmark returning 3.45%, as gains in South Korea and Japan were offset by weakness in China.

Throughout 2025, international equities outperformed their US counterparts, defying widespread expectations of continued US leadership. A declining dollar provided a helpful tailwind to international returns, while regional, sector-specific and policy-driven factors supported a broader set of market leaders. AI infrastructure investment in Asia and optimism around European fiscal spending were especially noteworthy.

  • The Swiss market, heavily weighted toward health care and consumer staples, was seen as a safe haven amid global growth concerns and benefited from improving trade sentiment with the US.
  • In the UK, the Autumn Budget delivered a clearer fiscal policy roadmap and avoided feared sector-specific levies. These developments, along with attractive equity valuations, supported investor interest.
  • South Korean stocks gained on progress related to corporate governance initiatives and improved supply/demand picture for its memory chip companies, as manufacturers reported their capacity is largely sold out through 2026.
  • Japanese stocks slightly lagged the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index but were still positive, as optimism about renewed fiscal spending and continued corporate governance reform were slightly offset by monetary policy tightening.
  • Chinese stocks declined as the impact of front-loaded exports ahead of US tariffs faded and property-related wealth effects remained negative.
  • Taiwan's chip foundry, packaging and adjacent industries saw strong growth in 2025, supported by robust demand that shows no signs of slowing in 2026.
  • Chinese companies are less integrated into the global semiconductor ecosystem, but AI-related stocks in the country performed well as growing domestic activity drove performance.
  • Japanese companies play a pivotal role in the semiconductor supply chain via their chipmaking equipment and other key production materials, and these companies benefited from rising capital expenditures across industry.
  • Japanese stocks were also supported by the country's corporate governance reform, which continued to show signs of tangible progress. Companies made notable strides in simplifying corporate structures, improving capital efficiency and prioritizing shareholder returns.
  • European banks, supported by cheap valuations, improved capital positions and a more normalized interest rate environment, helped drive strong stock performance for the year.
  • Germany passed a robust fiscal spending package early in 2025, which supported equities in Europe's largest economy and provided a lift for more cyclical companies with cheap valuations throughout the region.
  • Military defense and adjacent firms also performed well as governments moved toward greater strategic autonomy from the US — expanding confidence in the sectors' future growth prospects.

While we monitor policy and geopolitical developments, they do not dictate our investment focus. We are drawn to companies where market prices have materially diverged from intrinsic value. Periods of volatility often create opportunities to invest in strong businesses that are temporarily mispriced. This disciplined approach keeps us focused on fundamentals and long-term value, rather than short-term market noise.

Key contributors

  • Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), a global memory chip manufacturer, benefited from improved sentiment around its high-bandwidth memory products, which began shipping to AI providers. Strengthening pricing in its traditional memory business also supported margins and contributed to share price gains.
  • Sumitomo Densetsu (SDNSF), a Japanese electrical contractor, benefited from a takeover bid by Daiwa House, whose tender offer at a premium price drove the stock higher.
  • Allfunds Group (AFNDY), a leading European wealth technology platform, benefited from a takeover offer submitted by Deutsche Börse, which lifted shares on expectations of a strategic acquisition.

Key detractors

  • uniQure (QURE), a pioneer in gene therapy, saw its shares decline following regulatory setbacks for its Huntington's disease candidate, AMT-130, as the FDA indicated that current data would be insufficient for an accelerated approval. The resulting delay in the commercial timeline caused a downward revision of the company's near-term valuation.
  • Alibaba Group (BABA), a leader in global e-commerce, underperformed as its core Chinese retail divisions faced stagnant growth amid cautious consumer spending. Profitability was also pressured by sizeable investments in AI infrastructure.
  • EXOR (EXXRF), a diversified European holding company, underperformed primarily due to weakness in Ferrari shares, one of its most significant holdings. Ferrari shares lagged after the company reduced its guidance at its Capital Markets Day, which weighed on EXOR's look-through value and investor sentiment.

New positions

  • SK hynix (HXSC.F), a leading South Korean memory chip manufacturer, operates in a consolidated industry. The company is benefiting from increased demand for higher-value products, such as high-bandwidth memory used in data centers and AI applications. The stock's valuation still reflects that of a traditional memory cycle rather than a sustained shift toward higher-value products.
  • Global contract research organization ICON (ICLR)— offers end-to-end clinical services across pharma, biotech and medical devices. We expect continued industry outsourcing to support growth and market share gains versus smaller providers.
  • NatWest Group (NWG) is a UK bank with a simplified, digitally oriented operating model following a multi-year restructuring. We initiated a position as the current valuation does not reflect improvements in the business, a steadier earnings profile and strong capital return potential.
  • European defense supplier Theon International (TNIPF) specializes in night vision, thermal imaging, and intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance systems. This founder-led, asset-light business is benefiting from a multi-year European rearmament trend. The current stock price is undemanding relative to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Eliminated positions

  • We exited our position in global industrial machinery supplier Konecranes (KNCRF) as shares reached our estimate of intrinsic value.
  • Shares of India-based textile manufacturer Arvind and Sweden-based mining and infrastructure equipment provider Epiroc (EPOAY) were sold to fund higher-conviction opportunities.
  • Global pharma packaging and device maker with exposure to biologics and GLP-1s Gerresheimer (GRRMF) was sold to realize tax losses.
  • Shares of Japanese electrical contractor Sumitomo Densetsu were sold following Daiwa House's tender offer at a premium above both the share price and our estimate of intrinsic value.
